{{Chess Opening Theory/Position |Accelerated Panov attack |eco=[[Chess/ECOB|B10]] |parent=
* [[../|Caro-Kann]] * [[Chess Opening Theory/1. c4|English]] → [[Chess Opening Theory/1. c4/1...c6|1...c6]] (transposition) }} == 2. c4 · Accelerated Panov attack == With pawns on c4 and e4, White locks down d5 square. This is a rare, aggressive sideline against the Caro-Kann. It is "accelerated" over the regular Panov where White plays [[Chess Opening Theory/1. e4/1...c6/2. d4/2...d5/3. exd5/3...cxd5/4. c4|c4 only after 2. d4 d5 3. exd5 cxd5]]. This can lead to very different positions. A difference is that, in the accelerated Panov, White leaves their pawn on d2 until after they have castled, so they may place their knight on c3 without it becoming pinned. [[/2...d5|'''2...d5''']], the main line, leads to a series of pawn trades: 3. cxd5 cxd5 4. exd5 Nf6 (4...Qxd5?! 5. Nc3! gains tempo on the queen). White is temporarily up a pawn but usually allows Black to take it back with 5. Nc3 Nxd5. In the resulting position, White is left with an [[w:Isolated pawn|isolated d-pawn]]. This is a weakness in an endgame, so it's important for White to capitalise on their middlegame chances, using the open lines created by the removal of their c- and e-pawns. White can make a play to keep the extra material with the intermezzo 5. Bb5+, to force the knight or bishop to d7 and so obstruct Black's queen's defence of the pawn. However, this line usually results in a queen trade (5...Nbd7 6. Nc3 a6 7. Qa4 Rb8 8. Bxd7+ Qxd7 9. Qxd7+ Bxd7{{chess/not}}) and in an endgame with doubled isolated d-pawns White may not feel the advantage of the extra material. [[/2...e5|'''2...e5''']] is a sideline called the '''open variation'''. It's a bit like playing an open game (1. e4 e5), except now after 3. Nf3 Black can't defend the pawn with ...Nc6 because their pawn is misplaced on c6. ==Theory table== {{Chess Opening Theory/Table}} :'''1.e4 c6 c4'''
